insights: Cost Reduction goals across all departments take 2.5–3.0× longer to complete than other goal categories (256–285 days vs. 75–100 days), confirming the duration anomaly is category-wide—not Finance-specific.

Finance’s Cost Reduction goals (258.9 days) are actually 4.1% shorter than the cross-department average for Cost Reduction (270.0 days), indicating Finance is performing relatively better—not worse—on this category.

The 2.88× duration gap for Cost Reduction goals persists uniformly across all priority levels, states, completion statuses, target percentages, and owner workloads, ruling out operational, motivational, or assignment-based explanations within the available data.

Description text analysis shows no meaningful difference in length, complexity, or thematic content between Cost Reduction and other goals—eliminating linguistic or definitional factors as drivers of prolonged duration.

The consistent multi-quarter increase in Cost Reduction goal durations within 2022 (184 → 354 days) suggests a time-dependent external pressure—such as tightening budget cycles, layered approval requirements, or evolving compliance constraints—not captured in current goal metadata.

All major internal drivers tested (priority, state, ownership, progress status, target ambition, textual scope) show negligible correlation with duration (|r| < 0.12), strongly indicating that root causes reside in unobserved process-level factors: e.g., cross-functional approvals, fiscal calendar dependencies, or governance handoffs outside the goal-tracking system.
summary: The core issue is not a Finance-specific failure but a systemic, category-wide delay in Cost Reduction goal execution—spanning all departments and persisting across all measured operational dimensions. Cost Reduction goals take over 2.5× longer than other categories, yet Finance performs slightly better than peers, undermining assumptions of departmental underperformance. No internal goal attributes—including priority, state, owner load, or textual description—explain the gap, confirming the cause lies outside the current dataset: most likely in external budgetary, approval, or compliance processes. The sharp Q1→Q4 duration rise within 2022 further points to time-bound organizational constraints rather than behavioral or structural flaws in goal management. Next focus should shift to integrating finance workflow logs, budget cycle calendars, and cross-departmental approval records to isolate procedural bottlenecks.
